coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of doped CxNy QDs as photoluminescent matrix for direct detection of hydroquinone
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jaiswal, Aniruddha
Kumar, Ashish
Prakash, Rajiv
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we are reporting facile hydrothermal synthesis of a highly photoluminescent doped carbon nitride quantum dots (CxNyQDs) and implied it for direct detection of hydroquinone (H2Q) by photoluminescence quenching phenomenon. Oxygen and sulphur moieties are regarded as dopant species in CxNyQDs and sourced from cheap solid precursors viz. cysteine and maleic acid. Morphological studies of CxNyQDs have done by SEM and TEM techniques, while structural analysis has carried out using FTIR, XPS, EDS and UV–Visible spectroscopy. The strong tendency of dispersivity of this QD in water has revealed by its zeta potential value of −32.4 mV. Optical properties of the as-prepared QDs have optimized at different excitation wavelengths. The photoluminescence stability of the dispersion is tested in various pH solutions and under continuous UV irradiation (365 nm). After that, sensing property is observed in quenching of photoluminescence feature of as-prepared QDs by direct addition of various concentrations of H2Q. We obtained lower detection limit (LOD) of 50 nM (S/N = 3) in linear range from 12 to 57.5 μM. The reduction in photoluminescence of QDs may be attributed to electron transfer from QDs to oxidized H2Q via -S− and -COO− groups present at its surface. Further, as-prepared QDs matrix exhibited high selectivity for hydroquinone over a range of potential interfering agents. Thus, the present work shows cost-effective facile synthesis of highly stable O- and S-doped carbon nitride (CxNy) quantum dots as promising photoluminescent sensor for pollutant hydroquinone without help of any enzyme or polymer assisted system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride quantum dot (CxNyQDs)
Hydroquinone detection
Photoluminescence
Interference
Limit of detection (LOD)

Excitation dependant PL intensity of QDs
pH dependence of PL intensity of QDs
Selectivityof O- and S-doped CxNyQDs and real sample analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are thankful to Dr. Chandan Upadhyay, School of Materials Science and Technology, IIT (BHU) for providing photoluminescence facility. Aniruddha Jaiswal is also thankful to Nandita Jaiswal and Dr. Ida Tiwari, Department of Chemistry, BHU for her support in application part of the research work and Council of Scientific and Industrial Research ( CSIR ), New Delhi, India, (File No. 09/1217(0016)/2017-EMR-I ). Authors are also thankful to the Central Instrument Facility, IIT (BHU) for SEM, TEM and FT-IR facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for synthesis of O- and S-doped CxNyQDs. Inset shows aqueous dispersion of as-prepared CxNy QDs.
(a) TEM image and (b) the corresponding particle size distribution histogram of CM61.

(a) UV–Vis absorbance (blue line) and PL spectra (red line), (b) FT-IR spectrum and (c) zeta potentials curve of as-prepared CM61 QDs. Inset of panel a shows photograph of CM61 QD dispersion under UV light at wavelength 365 nm.
XPS survey spectrum (a) and deconvoluted individual elemental peaks of as-prepared CM61 QD for C1s (b), N1s (c), O1s (d) and S2p (e).

(a) Emission spectra of CM61 at excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 420 nm, (b) plot of emission peak position corresponding to excitation wavelength, (c) PL intensity of CM61 at various pHs and (d) variation of PL intensity with pH value.
PL emission intensity of prepared sample under continuous irradiation of UV light (365 nm).

(a) PL emission spectrum of CM61 upon addition of various concentrations of H2Q solution; inset of panel a shows the zoomed part of reduction in PL intensity and (b) relationship between (Fo-F)/Fo and concentration of H2Q; inset of panel b exhibits the linear calibration plot in range of 12.0 to 57.5 μM.

Selectivity of the CM61 for 8 μM H2Q detection in presence of various interfering agents. [Concentration of metal ions Co2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, As2+ and Zn2+are 1 g/l; Sn2+, Cr3+ and Glu have concentrations of 5 mM; Ni2+and Hg2+ have concentrations of 0.1 g/l; o-Aminobenzoic acid (BA) and 4-Nitrophenol (4NP) have concentrations of 0.05 mM.]
Plausible mechanism of H2Q detection using CM61 QDs.
Quantum yields of CxNyQDs prepared by various molar ratios of precursors.

row.entry: Integrated emission intensity at λex = 360 nm

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different methods for detection of H2Q.
Determination of H2Q in real samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of doped CxNy QDs as photoluminescent matrix for direct detection of hydroquinone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University), Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Technology
Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University), Varanasi-221005 India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we are reporting facile hydrothermal synthesis of a highly photoluminescent doped carbon nitride quantum dots (CxNyQDs) and implied it for direct detection of hydroquinone (H2Q) by photoluminescence quenching phenomenon. Oxygen and sulphur moieties are regarded as dopant species in CxNyQDs and sourced from cheap solid precursors viz. cysteine and maleic acid. Morphological studies of CxNyQDs have done by SEM and TEM techniques, while structural analysis has carried out using FTIR, XPS, EDS and UV–Visible spectroscopy. The strong tendency of dispersivity of this QD in water has revealed by its zeta potential value of −32.4 mV. Optical properties of the as-prepared QDs have optimized at different excitation wavelengths. The photoluminescence stability of the dispersion is tested in various pH solutions and under continuous UV irradiation (365 nm). After that, sensing property is observed in quenching of photoluminescence feature of as-prepared QDs by direct addition of various concentrations of H2Q. We obtained lower detection limit (LOD) of 50 nM (S/N = 3) in linear range from 12 to 57.5 μM. The reduction in photoluminescence of QDs may be attributed to electron transfer from QDs to oxidized H2Q via -S− and -COO− groups present at its surface. Further, as-prepared QDs matrix exhibited high selectivity for hydroquinone over a range of potential interfering agents. Thus, the present work shows cost-effective facile synthesis of highly stable O- and S-doped carbon nitride (CxNy) quantum dots as promising photoluminescent sensor for pollutant hydroquinone without help of any enzyme or polymer assisted system.

Photoluminescence quenching of doped CxNy quantum dots by hydroquinone (H2Q) in presence of various contaminants.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic compounds are one of the organic pollutants which are released in water bodies as waste materials. These compounds are detrimental to aquatic bodies as well as aquatic species living in them. Hydroquinone (H2Q) is one of them that is carcinogenic in nature and resistance to biotic and non-biotic degradations [1,2]. It is listed as primary pollutant by European Union (EU) and US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) because it produces ROS (reactive oxygen species) in redox process which is cell damaging [3,4]. That is why, sensitive and selective detection of such pollutant is required to protect our eco-system. Some detection methods like electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [5], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], gas chromatography (GC) [7], voltammetry [8–10], flow injection analysis [11] etc. are available in earlier reports, however, detection methods based on absorbance have much advantage over these one due to quick response and high sensitivity [12,13]. Few reports are available on semiconducting QDs (e.g. CdSe QD) and its hybrid system (e.g. CdTe QD-enzyme hybrid; conjugated polymer-enzyme hybrid) for fluorescent detection of H2Q [14–16]. However, these approaches have many disadvantages like complex sample preparation steps, enzyme denaturation and high limit of detection.

Visible light active graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been explored in a plethora of applications such as organic dye degradation, water splitting, super capacitor application, H2O2 production, CO2 and nitro reduction [17–19]. Carbon nitride at nanometric level is attractive destination because of its optical properties at zero dimension and one dimension level. Zero dimension of graphitic carbon nitride exists as carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs). CxNy quantum dots term is used for the non-stoichiometric ratios of sp2 hybridized carbon and nitrogen, where precursors used are not conventional as used in preparation of g-C3N4. CNQDs are ahead to traditional semiconductors based QDs owing to their low cost synthesis, non-toxicity and environment friendliness [20–26,45,46]. J. Chen et al. proposed detection of hydroquinone using combination of optical properties of graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots and specificity of enzymatic reaction [41]. There are two most prevailed precursors based on sources to prepare QDs (a) chemical and (b) biological precursors such as tree leaves, bark etc. It involves doping of non metals like O, N, P, S and B to enhance the fluorescence property of as-prepared QDs. These QDs are successfully used for heavy metals and biomolecules sensing either by colorimetric or fluorometric sensing methods [27,28,44].

Herein the present work, we are reporting the synthesis of photoluminescent O- and S-doped CxNy QDs by one step hydrothermal treatment with the help of cheap and bio-compatible precursors namely cysteine and maleic acid in optimum ratio at 150 °C. Here, cysteine is used as source of nitrogen and also provide dopant S; while maleic acid serves as stabilizing agent due to presence of carboxyl groups. The as-synthesized QD has high stability in aqueous media owing to its negatively charged surface. After that, it is further implied in sensitive and selective detection of H2Q by photoluminescence quenching phenomenon by virtue of electron transfer from QDs to H2Q.

L-Cysteine (extra pure 99%) was purchased from SRL chemicals and maleic acid (99%) was purchased from ACROS Organics. For interference study, standard metal ions solutions (conc. 1.0 g/L) of AAS (atomic absorption spectroscopy) category were purchased from SRL chemicals. Ultrapure water (Milli-Q Ultra-Pure System, Millipore, USA) was used for all synthesis and application part. All the chemicals were used as such without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of O-and S-doped CxNy QDs

ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis spectra and all photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on BioTek EPOCH2 and F-4600 FL Spectrophotometers respectively in 1 × 1 × 3 cm3 cuvette. Vibration spectrum (FT-IR) was recorded from a Thermo Scientific NICOLET iS5 instrument. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) images were captured on FEI NOVA NANO SEM 450 instrument. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were captured on FEI, TECHNAI G2 20 TWIN instrument with accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Zeta potential measurement was carried out on Horiba nanopartica SZ-100 series model. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) measurement was performed on Thermo Fischer Scientific ESCALAB Xi+.

Fig. 2a is the TEM image of the as-synthesized CM61, it is exemplified that CxNy QD is homogeneous in nature and exist in nanometric range. From corresponding particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 2b), it is confirmed that as-prepared QDs size ranging from 1 to 4 nm with the dominancy of particles having 2 nm size. However, FESEM image (see Fig. S2) shows globular morphologies of relatively larger size due to the agglomeration of dots. On the other hand, energy dispersive spectra (EDS) confirm for the presence of O and S as expected as dopant and C and N as part of CxNy in as-prepared QDs. Thereafter, Absorbance (blue curve) and emission (red curve) property of CM61 were measured with the help of UV–Visible and PL spectroscopy (Fig. 3a). Absorbance curve shows a strong absorption peak centered around 280 nm due to n → π* transition characteristics of CO [28,29]. Similarly, emission curve is centered at 439 nm, when excited by 360 nm wavelength. Inset of Fig. 3 shows photograph of aqueous dispersion of CM61 QDs when allowed to absorb UV radiation of long wavelength (365 nm) in the UV chamber exhibiting bright blue color emission.

Vibrational spectra of as-prepared CM61 confirmed the existence of O- and S- as dopant in CxNy (Fig. 3b). The vibration peaks at 757.31 cm−1 and 1381.11 to 1709.01 cm−1 confirm the presence of various CN units. The small peak at 2358.71 cm−1 corresponds to SH stretching showing small amount of sulphur is still present in the CxNy dots as dopant. The peak at 3400.43 cm−1 belongs to –OH of maleic acid and some moisture contents in the sample. The vibration peak at 2914.20 cm−1 is assigned to -NH2 stretching mode [23,25].

The ease of dispersivity was measured by measuring Zeta potentials (see Fig. 3c). The value of zeta potential was measured as −32.4 mV which reveals negative surface charge owing to presence of –COO−, -O− and small doped amount of –S−. This high magnitude of zeta potential causes the stability of the dots in water dispersion even for several months and also imparts significant stability comparable to earlier synthesized quantum dots [47].

XPS measurement was carried out to ensure the bonding nature of carbon, nitrogen and dopant elements in the prepared material. Survey spectrum (Fig. 4a) confirms about the existence of as-expected elements O, S, C and N in QDs. All the corresponding peaks were deconvoluted in order to remark respective bonding states (see Fig. 4b to e). C1s peak was deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.3, 285.1, 287.7 and 289.3 eV corresponding to CC, C-C/C-H, C-OH/C-O-C and CO groups respectively. N1s peak was deconvoluted as pyridinic/pyrrolic N moieties at 399.5 eV, while O1s peak was deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.0 and 531.8 eVcorresponding to CO and CO bonding state. S2p peak spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks 163.1, 164.3 and 164.5 assigned to -S-, -C=S- and residual –SH bonding state respectively [30]. Elemental composition (see Table S2) determined from peak area integration of XPS survey spectrum shows significant amount of sulphur and oxygen present in quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excitation dependant PL intensity of QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Excitation dependant PL spectrum was recorded in order to study the optical properties of the as-prepared QDs. We found that emission peaks shift its position and intensity upon excitation of sample at various excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 420 nm. The emission peaks first shift to shorter wavelength with increase in intensity below 340 nm. However, after this excitation wavelength, peak positions shift to higher wavelength with decrease in intensity (cf. Fig. 5a and b). Since, we are getting highest emission peak intensity at excitation wavelength 360 nm, this excitation wavelength is further used for all H2Q detection part. Such behavioral of emission spectra is attributed to various surface energy traps present on the surface of QDs and suggested mechanism for luminescence is based on radiative recombination of excitons [31,32]. Apart from this, large surface to volume ratios also play role for strong photoluminescence of as-prepared QDs that led to quantum confinement of emissive energy traps to the particle surface. While in case of larger particles there is absence of photoluminescence owing to small surface to volume ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH dependence of PL intensity of QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dependency of PL intensity was further checked by varying the pHs of aqueous dispersion media. There is no much variation in PL intensity of QDs were observed on varying pH of the solution. Although at pH 7.0, we observed better intensity maxima (cf. Fig. 5c and d). Similarly, long term PL stability of as-prepared QDs were tested by continuously irradiating the sample kept in cuvette under UV lamp (λ = 365 nm) for time period of 6 h as shown in Fig. 6 . We did not get any variation in PL intensity, which implies its long term PL stability under exposure conditions.

Control experiment was performed in order to check the individual role of cysteine and maleic acid in synthesis and PL activity of CxNyQDs. For this, first of all, maleic acid and cysteine were heated individually at the same temperature and same time period (as explained in synthesis section). In cysteine, little charring was observed, while maleic acid melts at the end of experimental conditions. Both these samples have no any fluorescence activity under above conditions. After that, mixture of these precursors in 6:1 molar ratio was again kept as such at room temperature for 48 h. No reaction takes place at the end of experimental conditions, which justifies the role of heat treatment in preparation of O- and S-doped CxNyQDs. As per above exemplifications, we choose 360 nm as excitation wavelength, aqueous dispersion agent having pH = 7.4 and CM61 as-prepared QDs for H2Q sensing alone or in presence of interfering media.

The detection of carcinogenic pollutant H2Q was performed with the help of as-prepared O- and S-doped CxNyQDs by photoluminescence quenching phenomenon. All the detection experiments were performed in 7.4 pH phosphate buffer solution (PBS). Excitation and emission slit width was kept 5.0 nm for all runs. For a typical detection, 2 ml PBS buffer was taken and certain amount of fixed concentration of QDs was added into it. Thereafter, various concentration of H2Q was spiked into it and PL intensity was recorded from the instrument at excitation λ = 360 nm. For interference test, individual run was carried out with individual addition of interfering agents; similar method was adopted for real sample analysis. To ensure the reliability of result, all the measurements were performed in triplicate at room temperature. Fig. 7a shows the variation in PL intensity of O- and S-doped CxNyQDs upon addition of different concentrations of H2Q solution from 0.05 μM to 57.50 μM (0.05, 0.33, 0.64, 1.28, 2.13, 3.48, 5.38, 8.0, 12.0, 20.0, 25.5, 39.5, 48.0 and 57.5 μM). Herein, it was observed that PL intensity of CxNy QDs is directly related to H2Q concentrations. Fig. 7b shows the relationship between PL quenching in terms of (Fo-F)/Fo and H2Q concentration, where Fo is PL intensity in absence of H2Q and F is PL intensity in presence of H2Q. Inset of Fig. 7b is linear calibration plot for H2Q detection in concentration range of 12.0 to 57.5 μM. The linear regression equation obtained is (Fo-F)/Fo = −0.070 + 0.021 [H2Q] (μM) with correlation coefficient of 0.9793. Limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as three times of standard deviation (S/N = 3; SI Eq. (2)). The obtained LOD is 50 nM which is comparatively much good to other electrochemical and fluorometric detection method of H2Q as listed in Table 2 . This detection limit is significant lower to the bar (0.5 mg/l) set by US Environmental Protection Agency for discharge of phenolic pollutants into water. Table 2 sums up the limit of detection (LOD), linear range, system deployed and techniques used along with references for detection of H2Q. It is clear that our proposed system has impressive sensitivity in detection of H2Q.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivityof O- and S-doped CxNyQDs and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of any sensor is an important parameter to test the onsite samples due to presence of various contaminants that might inhibit the existing sensing performance. We investigated the selectivity of as-prepared QDs for the detection of H2Q (8 μM) against a series of potential interfering ions and compounds found in waste water. Interfering ions chosen are Co2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, As2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Hg2+and compounds are Glucose, o-Aminobenzoic Acid (BA) and 4-Nitrophenol (4NP). Potential interfering agents (Ni2+ and Hg2+) diluted to 10 times show a small interference as compared to others. As seen in Fig. 8 , PL activity of as-prepared CxNyQDs has almost no impact on the presence of interfering agents during selective sensing of H2Q. Hence, as-prepared QDs show highly selective quenching of pollutant H2Q in presence of other interfering agents, which enhances its probability to be chosen as potential candidate for H2Q detection in real samples.

To test the feasibility of the as-prepared QDs in real life sensing system for detection of H2Q, we used four real samples in this regard namely Ganga Water I (collected from Ganga river, Haridwar), Ganga Water II (collected from Ganga river, Assi, Varanasi), Pond water (collected from pond near director office, IIT-BHU) and Tap water from (available in lab). We diluted these real samples ten times by adding pH = 7.4 PBS buffer into it and spiked 5 μM of H2Q into it. Table 3 shows the H2Q amount found in the real samples with % recovery and relative standard deviation for triplicate measurements. From this table, it is exemplified that our as-prepared QD is successfully able to intentionally added H2Q in all real samples.

Fluorescence based quenching is obtained either because of static quenching, dynamic quenching, photo induced electron transfer (PET), resonance energy transfer (RET), charge transfer (CT), electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bond interactions and pi-pi interactions or combination of these [43]. In earlier reported research work, mechanism of fluorescence quenching of QDs based sensing system is attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), electron transfer (ET) or other interactions at surface of dots [38]. Since, absorption spectrum of H2Q does not overlap to emission spectrum of as-prepared CxNyQDs, so possibility of FRET is ruled out (see Fig. S3). In our case, we propose that the photoluminescence quenching of QDs, upon introduction of H2Q, is due to electron transfer occurring from QDs to oxidized H2Q via –S− and -COO− groups available at the QDs surface, which results in reduction of PL intensity. H2Q remains oxidized into benzoquinone (BQ) in alkaline 7.4 pH PBS buffer by dissolved oxygen in presence of QDs [40]. Actually in this process, it has been interpreted that upon adhering of BQ to QDs' surface, BQ acting as e− acceptor promotes electron transfer from conduction band of QDs to its valence band resulting in quenching of PL intensity. Fig. 9 shows schematic illustration of mechanism involved in detection of H2Q by prepared CxNy QDs.

Our proposed method of detection of H2Q has edge over earlier reported methods in terms that here doped CxNy QDs senses H2Q directly without help of any enzyme or polymer assisted system with detection limit of 50 nM in linear range of 12.0 to 57.5 μM. The sensing material QDs were prepared in one step and shows stability of several months. The proposed sensing system also shows high level of H2Q sensing in different real water samples with RSD of 3.15%.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized highly stable photoluminescent O- and S-doped CxNyQDs which is efficient for sensitive and selective detection of H2Q at detection limit of 50 nM. It shows detection of H2Q by electron transfer mechanism between QDs and H2Q that result in quenching of PL intensity of dots. The as-prepared QDs dispersion is stable for months owing to high zeta potential value of −32.4 mV. At the same time, the PL intensity of QDs is stable in various pH values and under continuous exposure of UV light. The effectiveness of detection was tested in presence various potential interfering ions and compounds. Real sample test was also done to check the reliability of proposed method and result obtained are 98–102% range of recovery with 3.15% of relative standard deviation. The proposed system is facile in synthesis, highly stable, sensitive and selective for detection of pollutant H2Q in real life samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Authors are thankful to Dr. Chandan Upadhyay, School of Materials Science and Technology, IIT (BHU) for providing photoluminescence facility. Aniruddha Jaiswal is also thankful to Nandita Jaiswal and Dr. Ida Tiwari, Department of Chemistry, BHU for her support in application part of the research work and Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), New Delhi, India, (File No. 09/1217(0016)/2017-EMR-I). Authors are also thankful to the Central Instrument Facility, IIT (BHU) for SEM, TEM and FT-IR facility.

Aniruddha Jaiswal performed the experiments and measurements. Ashish Kumar helped in experiments. Aniruddha Jaiswal and Ashish Kumar involved in manuscript writing. The concept of the work was of Rajiv Prakash and work conducted under his guidance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119019.
